The Places That Stay With You

lesson.menu_title: The Places That Stay With You -- People and Places That Made Your Trip
lesson.menu_desc: Reading and describing memorable places with relative clauses -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- The Places That Stay With You
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 14 -- The Places That Stay With You
phases: The Places That Stay With You

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a famous place that is easy to recognize
to stay in your memory, hard to forget
worn and marked by time and the weather
modest and simple, not trying to impress
very quiet and peaceful, with little happening
to become more likeable the more you know it

reading.rtitle: The Town That Stayed With Me
paras: Every traveler has one place that refuses to leave them. For me it is a town whose name I can barely pronounce, tucked into a fjord that most maps do not bother to mark. It was not the kind of place that appears on postcards. There were no famous landmarks, no museums that people queue for. And yet it is the town that stayed with me.

I had stumbled upon it by accident, on a bus whose driver spoke no English. I got off in a sleepy square where an old man was mending nets, and a woman who ran the only cafe waved me in. The cafe, which had just four tables, became my whole world for three days. It was there, over endless cups of coffee, that I met the people who would turn a place I had never heard of into somewhere I will never forget.

That is the strange thing about the places that stay with you. They are rarely the famous ones. More often they are the unassuming corners whose beauty sneaks up on you: a weathered harbor where the light does something no camera can catch, a bakery whose owner remembers your name on the second morning. A place does not stay with you because of what is there. It stays because of who was there, and how they made you feel.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel essay, The Places That Stay

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The best places to visit are the famous cities with the biggest landmarks.
The places that stay with us are usually small, unassuming ones, and they stay because of the people we meet there.
You should always plan your trips carefully and avoid getting lost.

items: The writer says the town is famous and appears on postcards.
He says there were no famous landmarks and it was not the kind of place that appears on postcards.
The writer found the town by accident.
He says he had stumbled upon it by accident, on a bus whose driver spoke no English.
The cafe was large and always crowded.
He says the cafe had just four tables and became his whole world for three days.
The writer says a place stays with you because of the people who were there.
He says a place stays because of who was there, and how they made you feel.

parts: he arrived, he got off in a square
an old man was mending nets. A woman
had just four tables, waved him in. It is the unassuming corners
beauty sneaks up on you that we never forget.

gapfill.text: This text is full of RELATIVE CLAUSES, which add information about a noun. Use WHO for people, WHICH for things, THAT for either (in defining clauses only), WHERE for places, WHEN for time, and WHOSE for possession. A DEFINING clause is essential and takes no commas: 'the town THAT stayed with me'. A NON-DEFINING clause adds extra information, takes commas, and cannot use that: 'Bergen, WHICH sits between seven mountains, is stunning'.

bank.label: Useful language for describing a place that stayed with you
items: It is a place that stayed with me for years.
It is a town whose streets are steep and cobbled.
It is the kind of corner where time slows down.
The people who live there made the whole trip.
It is an unassuming little place that few people know.
It is a harbor which the evening light transforms.
It is a bakery whose owner remembers your name.
It is somewhere that really grows on you.

lf.title: Defining or non-defining? (blue = non-defining: extra information, with commas)
items: sits between seven mountains, is stunning.
grew up there, showed me around.

lf.text: A DEFINING clause (A and C) is essential: it tells you which town, which cafe, so it takes no commas and can use that. A NON-DEFINING clause (B and D, in blue) just adds extra information: you could remove it and still know what is meant, so it takes commas and uses which or who, never that.

items: Describe a place that stayed with you. Use a defining relative clause: 'It is a town where...', 'It is the place that...'.
Describe a person who made a trip special. Use who and whose: 'She was a guide who...', 'a woman whose...'.
Describe your city to a visitor, adding extra information with a non-defining clause: 'Sao Paulo, which..., is...'.
Talk about a small, unassuming place you stumbled upon. Say what it was like and who was there, using relative clauses.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = that, 2 = when, 3 = where, 4 = who, 5 = which, 6 = whose.
Model (defining): 'It is a town where I spent three days that I will never forget.'
Model (non-defining): 'Bergen, which sits between seven mountains, stayed with me.'
Structure: who = people, which = things, that = defining only, where = places, when = time, whose = possession. Non-defining clauses take commas and cannot use that.
answerkey.note: Do not use 'what' as a relative pronoun: not 'the place what I loved' but 'the place that I loved'. And in a non-defining clause, use which or who, never that: 'Bergen, which...', not 'Bergen, that...'.

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it is Sigrid. You asked me which place I would go back to tomorrow if I could, and I have thought about it all day. It is not a city you would find in a guidebook. It is a tiny village on the west coast, whose harbor is so small that only three boats fit. There is a wooden house there, which my grandmother was born in, where the whole family still gathers every summer. And there is a baker, an old man who has run the same shop for fifty years, who still remembers the name of everyone who walks in. That is the place that stayed with me. Not because of what is there, but because of who is there. Come and see it one day. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the places that refuse to leave us, and why they are so rarely the famous ones. Here is something every traveler eventually notices. You visit the great cities, the ones that are printed on every postcard, and somehow they blur together. But then there is that one small town whose name nobody recognizes, the little square where you sat for an hour, the cafe whose owner poured you a second coffee for free. Those are the places that stay. And it is worth asking why. It is almost never the scenery. It is the people who were there, the stranger who gave you directions, the family who invited you in. A place becomes unforgettable not because of what is built there, but because of who you met, and how they made you feel. So the next time a place stays with you, look closely. Odds are there is a face behind it.
